canonical_definition: The behavior of stylistic variation is framed in two distinct ways across the provided literature. One perspective defines it as rephrasing a report using "different but semantically equivalent expressions" that do not alter the underlying clinical meaning, such as by reordering sentences ("D-CoDe: Scaling Image-PretrainedVLMs to Video via Dynamic Compression and Question Decomposition"). These are described as "purely stylistic or linguistic changes" that are clinically insignificant. A different framing, referred to as "Tone adjustment", characterizes the behavior more broadly as rewriting a given text into a "specific style" or different tonal register as specified by a task ("Principled Personas: Defining and Measuring the Intended Effects of Persona Prompting on Task Performance"). For measurement, stylistic variation is reported to be operationalized using LIWC, with the provided evidence describing a procedure of "length-controlled segmentation" where texts are divided into parts ("MultiMed-ST: Large-scale Many-to-many Multilingual Medical Speech Translation").
papers: D-CoDe: Scaling Image-PretrainedVLMs to Video via Dynamic Compression and Question Decomposition
Principled Personas: Defining and Measuring the Intended Effects of Persona Prompting on Task Performance
Video-RTS: Rethinking Reinforcement Learning and Test-Time Scaling for Efficient and Enhanced Video Reasoning

definitions.text: Rephrasing a report using different but semantically equivalent expressions, sentence order, or hedging without altering clinical meaning.
definitions.paper: D-CoDe: Scaling Image-PretrainedVLMs to Video via Dynamic Compression and Question Decomposition

definitions.text: Rewriting a given text in a different stylistic or tonal register as specified by the task.
definitions.paper: Principled Personas: Defining and Measuring the Intended Effects of Persona Prompting on Task Performance

relationships.paper: MultiMed-ST: Large-scale Many-to-many Multilingual Medical Speech Translation
relationships.evidence: “we also explore length-controlled segmentation: in C1, dividing texts into three equal parts, and in C2, sampling a body portion matching the average length of the introduction and conclusion.”

emnlp25: MultiMed-ST: Large-scale Many-to-many Multilingual Medical Speech Translation
